compressed_preview: Given that, I think we can realistically ship two of the four proposed features: the SSO integration and the bulk export tool. The real-time collaboration feature and the mobile offline mode will need to slip to Q4. So total first-year cost lands around $96,000. The system must handle at least 50,000 requests per second at the edge with p99 latency overhead under 2 milliseconds. Sliding window logs were rejected due to memory overhead — storing a timestamp per request for high-volume keys would require roughly 800 MB per million active keys, which exceeds our Redis budget. Enterprise tier: negotiated per contract, typically 50,000 to 200,000 requests per

Benchmarks run on a staging clone showed a 34% improvement in query latency for our top 20 slowest queries, largely due to improved query planning around our composite indexes. One concern: in `auth_service.py` line 142, you're storing the refresh token hash in Redis with no explicit expiration set on the key itself, only relying on the JWT's own exp claim. In `rotate_refresh_token()`, there's a race condition: if a client sends two concurrent requests with the same refresh token — say due to a retry after a network timeout — both could pass the validity check before eit
